chapters.title: The dark horse of human evolution
chapters.text:
Homo sapiens had a rough start. A hundred thousand years ago, we were far behind other human species and seemed unlikely to last. Homo erectus, in contrast, looked unbeatable. They were skilled hunters and inventors, the first to master fire, and the most widespread species on the planet.

Never lose hope. Your finish, not your start, decides how the journey ends.

Seventy-five thousand years ago, Neanderthals took the lead. These strong, skilled hunters ruled the Ice Age. They beat others not just in muscle but also in brain size, and even Homo sapiens could not top them. Neanderthals cared for their close kin, loved art and jewelry, and carried a gene tied to speech. While Homo sapiens fled the glaciers, Neanderthals adapted to the cold. On paper, they looked like the future.

Yet by forty thousand years ago, only Homo sapiens remained. Why?

Let us travel back in time to find out what kept us alive, and how to use that knowledge to bring our divided societies closer together. It is time to look at evolution from a new angle.

chapters.title: What dogs can teach us about being human
chapters.text:
When we talk about what sets us apart, we often mention motor skills or raw brainpower. Our small acts can be just as telling. From about nine months old, we understand what others mean when they point. Instead of staring at the finger, we draw an invisible line to the thing being pointed at.

Do not sell yourself short. Many things you take for granted are small miracles of nature.

On the surface, this kind of shared communication looks simple. Yet most species, including our closest relatives, the chimpanzees, cannot do it. They read our gestures easily when we threaten or compete with them. When we point to help, they stay lost. In one test, chimps knew that food was under one of two plates, but not which one. Even when a researcher pointed at the right plate, they kept guessing.

If our closest relatives fail the task, no animal can do it, the researchers concluded. One scientist disagreed and ran the same test with her dog, who passed easily. Daycare dogs and even six-week-old puppies passed too. The last case is striking, since puppies that young can barely walk. The fact that this skill shows up so early suggests it is genetic and key to survival.

How did dogs get it? Wolves lack the skill, so domestication is the most likely cause. For dogs, self-domestication is the better phrase. Wolves and Homo sapiens started to share space when humans were hunter-gatherers. It is unlikely that early people tamed wolves and shared their hard-earned meat. A more believable story is that some wolves lost their fear of hunters and wandered close to human camps to eat scraps. As fear gave way to interest, serotonin levels in the wolves rose, and the change began.

Like many other tamed species, the friendlier wolves picked up a set of traits. Their skin color stopped being useful as camouflage. Their brains and teeth shrank. Their tails curled. Their ears went soft and small. They kept juvenile behavior.

Modern dogs are the end point of that shift. Another change, the ability to breed all year rather than one season, helped their genes spread.

In short, we did not befriend wolves. They tamed themselves in relation to us. Can animals do this to their own kind? It is time to meet the bonobos.

chapters.title: Friendliness pays off
chapters.text:
Bonobos and our ancestors rarely met, but these apes carry many of the same traits as tamed animals. The contrast with their closest relatives, chimpanzees, makes it clear. Bonobos have smaller brains and faces, less skin pigment, juvenile behavior, and stronger social skills.

Those differences shape behavior. Male chimps, led by an alpha male, patrol their range and attack strangers when they have the numbers. Female chimps are not gentle either. Food is scarce, so a strict pecking order forms, and only high-ranking females eat well. Low-ranking females are left out and easy targets.

Bonobos do not have alpha males. Why? There is enough plant food for the whole group. Females do not need to fight over it, and they team up to protect each other from males that try to force them to mate or hurt their babies. No protective alpha male is needed.

As a result, the only way for males to mate is to ask their mothers to set up a meeting. Even then, they have a chance only if they are friendly. That is who female bonobos choose. Over time, that pressure shaped the species. Only the friendliest bonobos left descendants. Today, the species shares food with others and greets strangers with open arms.

Friendliness matters beyond bonobos. It is one of the strongest traits of all tamed animals, and its role is huge. The contrast between the white-rumped munia, a small bird from tropical Asia, and its tamed relatives shows the point. Wild birds learn songs only from their parents, while tamed birds pick up songs from other birds. Their openness gives them an edge.

Instead of fighting to be the most important person in the room, we might do better to aim at being the friendliest.

The story of self-tamed animals points to a bold idea. What if our ability to read each other and cooperate with people we barely know comes from self-domestication? In other words, what if we were shaped by pressure for friendliness?

A useful note: where female chimps sit in a tree signals their rank. The higher they sit, the higher their status.

chapters.title: The recipe for our survival
chapters.text:
Humans have always had an edge over other species. The human brain is not the largest, but its computing power beats the rest. The trick is neuron density.

For most mammals, a big brain comes at a cost. It stretches the neurons out, lowering density and the brain power that depends on it. Primates broke that pattern. As their brains grew, neuron density held, because the neurons kept their size. Compare a capybara and a rhesus macaque, which have similar brain sizes, and the macaque is far sharper and more in control.

The mix of brain size and density lifted human ability. Still, the rise was not smooth. When the human brain grew to three times the size of a chimp brain two hundred thousand years ago, we were not yet great at planning or reasoning. That came only about fifty thousand years later. In the meantime, some big-brained human species went extinct.

Being the smartest does not guarantee survival.

What tipped the scales around fifty thousand years ago and kicked off the cultural revolution? The answer is a wider social network. A group is the main support for weak members, and it is needed for any kind of technology. Cutting people off from the wider group not only stalls new tools, it also erodes skills that people already had.

At the same time, broad networks can spark conflict. How did Homo sapiens turn fights into shared purpose? According to Brian Hare and Vanessa Woods, our self-domestication, or friendliness toward a wider circle, was the turning point. It is more than a guess. Evidence from eighty thousand years ago shows that we picked up traits common to tamed species.

Skull size shrank. Some research links that to a rise in serotonin, a hormone tied to friendliness. The face became more juvenile, with smaller features, as testosterone levels fell. Pigment changed. The clearest sign is the white sclera, the white of the eye, which makes the iris stand out. When we look into someone else eyes for a while, a rush of oxytocin helps us feel good about them. Special neurons also fire when we see another person eyes, even with our side vision. That is how we can sense when someone is watching us.

Our past gives hints for living in better balance. How to apply them comes in the Try This section.

chapters.title: The dark side of friendliness
chapters.text:
If humans really tamed themselves, how did it happen? Multitasking genes give the answer. Some of them steer stem cells, deciding what kind of cell they will become. That gives them a lot of influence. A small change can lead to large new traits. The rest is the job of selection pressure.

For example, a shift in multitasking genes raised the friendliness of some Homo sapiens. Cooperation made their lives safer and longer, and they had more children. Over time, others followed their lead, since it raised their own odds of survival.

Yet, unlike bonobos, our species did not become friendlier to all strangers. We became friendlier to people inside our own group, people we see as sharing our identity. That is where the dark side of friendliness comes in.

Oxytocin shows the pattern. On one hand, it lowers our aggression toward people we see as part of our group. On the other hand, it makes us harsher toward people we see as a threat, or even a potential threat, to our family or identity. How? When we feel in danger, oxytocin shuts down the brain areas that handle empathy.

What sets off that defense? Some scientists say a threat to life or resources is a main cause. Dehumanization by another group is just as important. It takes many forms. In the 1800s, Europeans and Americans saw indigenous people as halfway between apes and humans. As a result, in the early 1900s, the Bronx Zoo in New York put a person on display.

Another form of dehumanization is calling someone a monkey, or simianization. Even though it spread during the slave trade, it was still used in the 1800s and 1900s against Black people of different nations, the Irish, Jewish people, and Japanese people.

Later, dehumanization took other forms, like calling people insane or illegal. As the psychologist Albert Bandura showed, that does not weaken the effect on group perception. In one of his 1970s studies, participants were told they could choose an electric shock as a possible punishment for workers, though no one was actually shocked. When the workers were described in dehumanizing terms, participants chose to double the voltage.

Ethnicity, gender, or looks does not make one person more human than another.

chapters.title: Old tools, new targets
chapters.text:
As the world grows more liberal, dehumanization should fade. Is it?

Imagine this. The American president invites both parties to a friendly dinner. Everyone has a good time and enjoys the company. The next day, Republicans and Democrats meet to talk about sports over snacks after a day of debate in Congress. Does it sound like a fantasy?

It may, but American politics looked like that only fifty years ago. Through cooperation, lawmakers passed major bills. The Civil Rights Act banned discrimination. Reagan tax plan cut taxes sharply.

Political rivals in a liberal democracy cannot afford to be enemies. ~ Brian Hare, PhD, Vanessa Woods

Then, in the 1990s, the Gingrich Revolution changed things. It was named for a congressman, Newt Gingrich, who thought voters would not back the other party if politics worked well. He set out to break Congress by killing bipartisanship. He did it in two main ways.

He limited cross-group friendships. He cut the Congress work week to three days, so representatives spent more time in their home districts. He pushed dehumanization of opponents. Republicans started calling critics decay and sick. Gingrich even called Democrats Nazis. That kicked off mirror dehumanization, where Democrats used similar labels back.

Social media has made dehumanization easier than ever. Anyone can spread hate speech and target a chosen audience with a few clicks. Will anyone be punished? It depends on the country. Germany, for example, has firm rules. In 2017, the government warned social media firms to delete hate speech or face a fifty-million-euro fine.

The United States has no clear legal definition of hate speech, which gives room for those who want to divide. Many efforts to limit hate speech fail, because critics see them as censorship.

Hate speech has another effect. It weakens trust in democracy. When there are many real or made-up conflicts, the public tends to want strong leaders. One person with huge power can make sweeping changes, but the cost is often human rights.

Democracy is not perfect, but it is the only system that values every human life and keeps checks on power. We have to learn how to build friendliness if we want to save it and bring communities together.

Mutual benefit matters more than being right.

chapters.title: Tolerance is something we can build
chapters.text:
One of the saddest examples of dehumanization took place during the Second World War, when Nazi propaganda spread lies about Jewish people. Even so, thousands risked their lives to save Jews. For a long time, scientists tried to find what those rescuers had in common. They came from different jobs, religions, incomes, and education levels.

Even in the darkest times, you can be the light.

Sociologists Samuel and Pearl Oliner finally found the answer. All the rescuers had Jewish playmates, friends, or coworkers. They knew who Jewish people really were from their own experience. That finding broke the myth that contact breeds conflict. In a safe setting, contact is the only way to grow tolerance.

A few settings offer regular friendly contact.

Schools and universities. Diversity is needed for a healthy society. Just having students of different races and ethnicities is not enough. Contact has to happen in a setting that is not competitive. How? One way is the jigsaw method. Teachers give each student a source to read and ask them to share the key points with the class. At the end, everyone writes a paper using the data each of them presented. The method makes students depend on each other, so they listen and ask questions. Another way is to make sure dorm roommates come from different genders, races, or ethnic backgrounds.

Housing. Even small talks with neighbors of different backgrounds raise tolerance. Authorities should fight bias in renting and lending. Everyone should have fair access to housing.

Public spaces. Many cities are filled with tall buildings, walls, and no green areas. People still need places to meet, like city gardens, playgrounds, and parks.

Culture. Contact with even fictional characters of different backgrounds builds empathy. Books, films, games, and cartoons should reflect the world as it really is, with all its variety. The novel Uncle Tom Cabin, for instance, helped push racial tolerance.

As psychologist Gordon Hodson has shown, talking with people from different backgrounds in settings that are not competitive can even make followers of extreme views more open.

Our lives should be measured not by how many enemies we have beaten, but by how many friends we have made. ~ Brian Hare, PhD, Vanessa Woods

chapters.title: Wrapping up: choose friendliness and reach across divides
chapters.text:
Brawn and brainpower look like the keys to survival. Yet the long story of our kind says the opposite. The ones who last are the friendliest.

Today, the lesson matters more than ever, and we have to dodge the traps set by people who push division. Disagreeing with our ideas does not mean attacking who we are. Different experiences do not mean we have nothing in common. In the end, we all share one identity: human.

Defending ourselves and our close people matters. Still, let us first make sure we are not tilting at windmills like Don Quixote, chasing enemies that are not really there. We can understand many more people once we hear their stories. Let us listen first, and then we will be heard.

Try this. Ready to bring out your friendlier side and shift how you connect with others? These steps can help you start today.

Find your friendliness strength. Think about a time your kindness helped solve a problem. Write down what you did and why it worked.

Create zones free of division. Set up virtual or physical spaces where divisive topics are off the table. Use those moments to celebrate shared interests, like art, nature, or community events.

Listen with care. Spend time really trying to understand someone else view by asking open-ended questions. Empathy grows when you focus on their story, not your reply.

Swap competitive games for cooperative ones. Working together builds teamwork and creative problem-solving.

Spend time on a prosocial act. Give fifteen minutes to helping someone, by giving advice, helping with a task, or simply checking in.

Host a diversity dinner. Invite people from different backgrounds to share a meal and trade stories. Food builds bridges, and talks at a table grow trust and understanding.

Practice strong gratitude. Thank someone for a quality or action you admire, even when you do not always agree. Appreciation can ease tension and deepen human bonds.

Bringing our shared humanity back into focus starts with small, deliberate actions. You have the power to make a difference, one connection at a time.
